attributes.stem: A 60-year-old man is reviewed on the morning ward round. He is day 3 post uncomplicated open nephrectomy for renal cell carcinoma. He feels slightly light-headed. He has an epidural catheter infusing fentanyl and bupivacaine. His pulse rate is 100/min, blood pressure 90/60mmHg, respiratory rate 19/min, and temperature 36.8C. He is cool and clammy peripherally. His urine output has been 25mL/hour over the last 24 hours. The drain from his nephrectomy site contains 50mL of fresh blood. He has a history of type 2 diabetes, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease, and gout. A bedside electrocardiogram shows sinus rhythm.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next step?
attributes.explanation:
This patient in postoperative care has signs and symptoms of hypovolaemic shock (hypotension, tachycardia, cold and clammy skin) and should be treated with resuscitative measures. The presence of fresh blood in the drain suggests active bleeding from the wound site. Thus the best next step should involve giving an intravenous fluid bolus to maintain his intravascular volume and blood pressure. Blood for transfusion should be arranged if the patient has persistent haemorrhagic shock and the site of bleeding should be investigated and addressed.

This patient needs immediate resuscitation and normalisation of his haemodynamic status. Intravenous normal saline at 100 mL/hr will not be sufficient to maintain his blood pressure and intravascular volume.

Vasopressors are not indicated in hypovolaemic shock since they do not treat the underlying problem and may exacerbate tissue ischaemia.

An epidural infusion is unlikely to be the cause of this patient’s haemorrhagic shock.

An arterial line is useful for investigative purposes (eg, continuous blood pressure monitoring, frequent blood sampling). Although accurate blood pressure measurement is desired to influence clinical decisions in patients with haemodynamic instability, it is not the best next step in patients at risk of severe haemodynamic collapse. Management should be focussed on treatment instead of monitoring.
learningPoints: The first action in hypovolaemic shock is to restore the circulating volume.
references: Gutierrez G, Reines H, Wulf-Gutierrez M. Clinical review: Hemorrhagic shock. Critical Care [Internet]. 2004 [cited 22 January 2021];8(5):373. Available from: https://ccforum.biomedcentral.com/articles/10.1186/cc2851
